characters.blurb: Shared the three-headed pact with Caesar and Crassus, married Julia, then fought Caesar’s civil war to death in Egypt.
Favorite whom Caesar spared and advanced; led the Ides conspiracy with Cassius and fell at Philippi.
Unyielding optimate who fought Caesar’s laws, fled after Pharsalus, and killed himself at Utica rather than take pardon.
Consulted, flattered, and disappointed; his consular prestige and letters frame Caesar’s civilian wars.
Seven-times consul whose memory Caesar revived against Sulla’s party—the inheritance of a populares name.
Master of the Horse and loyal civil-war captain; gave Caesar’s funeral speech and inherited his popular mantle.
Spared young Caesar reluctantly—“in that young man I see many Mariuses”—after the refusal to divorce Cornelia.

characters.relation: Rich partner of the first compact
characters.blurb: Bankrolled debts and politics until Carrhae killed him and broke the three-way balance with Pompey.
Lean, hungry conspirator who drew Brutus in; Caesar’s mistrust of his pale look is Plutarch’s omen.

characters.relation: Demagogue of the Bona Dea scandal
characters.blurb: Tried (they said) to reach Pompeia at the women’s rite; Caesar divorced her—“Caesar’s wife must be above suspicion.”
Married to Pompey to seal the alliance; her death in childbirth loosened the last personal tie between the dynasts.
Second wife, dismissed though perhaps innocent, when Clodius’s intrusion stained her household.
Warned Caesar not to go to the Senate; her fears and the omens fill the night before the murder.
Smuggled into Caesar’s quarters at Alexandria; her cause and Caesarion tied Rome’s civil war to the Nile.
Guided young Ptolemy against Cleopatra and insulted Caesar; executed when the palace war turned.
Commanded the forces that trapped Caesar in Alexandria after Pompey’s murder.
Rhetor who argued that killing Pompey would please Caesar—and so stained Egypt’s welcome.
Metellus Scipio, Cato’s ally, who held Africa against Caesar and fell after Thapsus.
Several Metelli oppose Caesar; the Life’s “Metellus” often marks that senatorial bloc beside Cato.

characters.relation: Consular enemy in the civil war
characters.blurb: Named among the Pompeian consulars who fled Rome and stiffened the Senate’s war party.
Domitius Ahenobarbus, early Pompeian opponent Caesar pardoned—then faced again in the Greek campaign.
Watched over Caesar’s house in the Bona Dea affair; a disciplined mother for a famously ambitious son.
Suebian leader Caesar beat early in the Gallic War—the first taste of northern kings as Roman clients or corpses.

characters.relation: Top legate who deserted to Pompey
characters.blurb: Right hand in Gaul who chose Pompey in 49 and died in the African or Spanish wars against his old chief.
Trusted in the civil war’s navy and politics; a Caesarian of the next generation.

characters.relation: Optimate who opposed his early offices
characters.blurb: Princeps senatus who fought Caesar’s bid for the chief pontificate and other populares moves.
Brought African cavalry against Caesar; defeated after Thapsus’s campaign wrecked the republican remnant.
Legate beaten in the Ilerda campaign when Caesar first broke Pompey’s Spanish armies.
Calpurnia’s father; consular connection that tied Caesar to a respectable optimate house even as he broke the Republic.
Co-consul of 59 who tried to void Caesar’s laws by omen-watching; Romans joked the year was of Julius and Caesar.
Brilliant spendthrift who carried Caesar’s vetoes in 50 and died for him in Africa.
Marian leader whose daughter Cornelia Caesar refused to divorce at Sulla’s order.

characters.relation: Conspirator he was whispered to favor
characters.blurb: Caesar’s mildness in the Catilinarian debate let enemies smear him; Plutarch weighs the suspicion carefully.
Ape of Cato who heckled the dynasts; comic relief in the Senate’s last years.
Named in Caesar’s will as adopted son—the Octavius who becomes Augustus after the Life’s curtain.

characters.relation: Greek pair in the Parallel Lives
characters.blurb: Alexander is his match: ambition measured against a world map, and a death that leaves the empire to quarreling heirs.

characters.relation: Conqueror, dictator, and subject of the Life
characters.blurb: Quaestor, pontifex maximus, consul, and dictator perpetuo—his ambition, clemency, and murder on the Ides of March made him the hinge on which the Republic turned into empire.
Daughter of the Marian leader Cinna; Caesar refused Sulla’s order to divorce her, risking proscription, and stayed loyal until her death left him free to marry Pompeia.

characters.relation: Optimate consul who opposed Caesar’s command extension
characters.blurb: Consul who joined Lentulus in blocking Caesar’s recall proposals; he struck a senator of New Comum to humiliate Caesar and send Pompey to war.

characters.relation: Historian and officer present at the Rubicon
characters.blurb: Caesar’s companion when he crossed the Rubicon; Pollio later recorded Caesar’s Latin words at that moment, which he himself translated into Greek in his own history.

characters.relation: Praetor before whom young Caesar pleaded
characters.blurb: Marcus Lucullus served as praetor of Macedonia when Caesar prosecuted Publius Antonius there; his namesake Lucius Lucullus appears in the Clodius scandal as Clodia’s husband.
Close supporter who managed Caesar’s business affairs; Plutarch cites Oppius’s own account of Caesar’s famous ability to dictate to several scribes simultaneously.

characters.relation: Veteran senator who joked about Caesar’s isolation
characters.blurb: Old man who told Caesar that the senators stayed away from the Forum out of fear of him; when Caesar asked why Considius himself came, the elder said age was his guard.

characters.relation: Fellow consul with Caesar; key ally
characters.blurb: Publius Servilius Vatia Isauricus, Caesar’s consular colleague after the first dictatorship; he sided with Caesar against those urging peace with Pompey.

characters.relation: Historian cited on the Ides omens
characters.blurb: Titus Livius, whose account of the augur Spurinna’s prediction and of Calpurnia’s dream Plutarch mentions when describing the omens before the Ides of March.
Son of Mithridates who overran Bithynia while Caesar was occupied in Egypt; Caesar defeated him at Zela so swiftly that he boasted ‘I came, I saw, I conquered.’
The battle of Pharsalus was won on the feast of Bacchus—the very day Pompey had four years before set out to war. Plutarch also notes the Bona Dea rites were kept for the mother of Bacchus.
Supreme Gallic commander whose father had been put to death for aiming at tyranny. He surrendered to Caesar at Alesia in full armor and was kept for the triumph.

locations.relation: Home of his rise and fall
locations.blurb: Birthplace, forum of his ambition, and the stage of the Ides—Caesar’s story begins and ends in Rome.
As a young man he lingered at Nicomedes’ court—gossip clung to the stay, and Sylla’s enmity hardened.

locations.relation: School of rhetoric after the pirates
locations.blurb: After ransoming himself from Cilician pirates, he studied with Apollonius and later crucified his captors as promised.

locations.relation: Spanish capital of his early command
locations.blurb: Seat of his Spanish service—quaestor and later propraetor—where he first tasted provincial power and debt’s pressure.
Nearly a decade of conquest, winter camps, and dispatches—the province that made him master of an army.
He threw a timber bridge across in days, showed the standards beyond the river, then broke the span behind him.
Twice he crossed with fleets—more reconnaissance and prestige than lasting conquest, yet Rome’s first look at the island.
Double lines of works closed on Vercingetorix; the surrender in armor sealed Gaul and Caesar’s military fame.
The little stream of Italy’s boundary—once crossed with the Thirteenth, the Republic’s quarrel became open war.
He seized it at once after the crossing; Italy’s towns opened as Pompey fell back toward Brundisium.

locations.relation: Adriatic gate in the chase of Pompey
locations.blurb: Harbor of embarkation: Pompey slipped east from here; Caesar followed into Greece when ships and season allowed.
Caesar’s entrenchments nearly penned Pompey, then broke; the setback sent him inland toward Thessaly.

locations.relation: Decisive battle of the civil war
locations.blurb: On the plain of Thessaly he broke Pompey’s larger army (48 BC); the Republic’s last great field army scattered.
He found Pompey already dead, stayed for the Alexandrian war, and left Cleopatra queen—and Caesarion rumored his.
Where he crushed Pharnaces so swiftly the dispatch ran: I came, I saw, I conquered.
He shattered the republican remnant in Africa (46 BC); Cato’s suicide at Utica followed the rout.

locations.relation: Last battle of the civil wars
locations.blurb: Hardest fight of all, against Pompey’s sons in Spain (45 BC)—after Munda, no rival army remained.
The homeland he crossed with legions after the Rubicon—towns chose sides as Pompey abandoned the west.
Province of his youth’s command and of Munda’s last blood—Spain bookends his military life outside Gaul.
Where the republican cause gathered under Scipio; Thapsus decided it, and Utica heard Cato’s end.
He came hunting Pompey and stayed to settle a dynasty—Egypt’s court became a second civil war.
The Greek East of his early wanderings—Bithynia, Rhodes, and the pirate coast before Rome claimed him fully.
He followed Pompey across the Adriatic into Greek lands—Dyrrhachium first, then the plain of Pharsalia.
Inland from the Adriatic camps—the open country where Caesar chose to risk everything against Pompey.

locations.relation: Eastern shore of the civil war
locations.blurb: Named among the eastern theaters as Pompey’s allies and Caesar’s pursuit shifted across the Greek north.
Mithridates’ old realm—Pharnaces rose there until Caesar’s lightning campaign ended at Zela.
On the map of the eastern war after Egypt—Caesar’s settlements and dispatches touched Syria’s coast.
Waters of the pirates who seized him as a young man—he vowed the cross, and kept the vow.
Not Caesar’s battlefield, but the city where Cato chose death rather than live under the victor of Thapsus.
The tribes across the Rhine—Caesar’s bridge was a show of force more than a lasting occupation.
Shorelands toward Dyrrhachium—Caesar’s crossing of the Adriatic opened the Greek half of the civil war.
Named with the eastern landings as Caesar’s forces gained the Illyrian coast opposite Italy.

locations.relation: Grain island on the war’s map
locations.blurb: A hinge of supply and passage between Italy and Africa in the years of civil war.
Counted among the western possessions whose loyalty mattered when Italy itself was in arms.
The mountain screen his Gallic command looked across—winter and war moved through their passes.
The great river of southern Gaul—corridor of allies, supplies, and early campaigns.
Named in the geography of northern Italy as Caesar’s province and later his march southward.
Rome’s river—backdrop to triumphs, floods of politics, and the city’s daily life under his dictatorship.
The mouth of the Tiber—Rome’s gate to the sea in an age when grain and fleets decided wars.

locations.relation: Colony he settled in the north
locations.blurb: A Cisalpine town tied to his patronage—Plutarch notes the colony among favors that bound the north to him.

locations.relation: Old African rival on the map
locations.blurb: The ruined Punic capital still haunted Roman memory when Caesar’s African war returned Rome to those shores.

locations.relation: Greek city on the war’s horizon
locations.blurb: Named among Greek places as the civil war’s eastern chapter moved through Hellenic lands.
Another Greek landmark in the geography of Pompey’s and Caesar’s contest across the Aegean world.
